title: Big O: Measuring Speed
subtitle: How we talk about code that's fast vs slow

sections.heading: The big idea
sections.body:
Two programs solve the same problem. One takes a second. The other takes an hour. Why?

**Big O notation** is the language we use to describe *how* the time grows as the input grows. Not exact seconds — just the *shape* of the growth.

sections.title: Finding a name in a phone book
sections.body:
If you flip through page by page, doubling the book doubles your work — that's **O(n)**, linear time.

If you open to the middle and pick a half, then the middle of that half — doubling the book only adds *one* extra step. That's **O(log n)**, logarithmic — wildly faster.

sections.caption: Growth rates compared, from best to worst

sections.heading: The rule of thumb
sections.body:
Count the loops. One loop over the input → **O(n)**. A loop inside a loop → **O(n²)**. No loops, just a direct access → **O(1)**.

We ignore constants. `O(2n + 10)` is just **O(n)**. We only care about the dominant shape as inputs get huge.

questions.question: What's the time complexity of accessing `arr[0]` in a JavaScript array?
questions.explanation: Indexing into an array is constant time — it doesn't matter how big the array is.

questions.question: A nested loop over an array of size n is:
questions.explanation: n iterations × n iterations = n² total operations.
